title: Saxo Bank Pricing Explained: All Plans, Costs & Fees (2026)

excerpt: Complete breakdown of Saxo Bank pricing — all plans, hidden fees, and how to save money.
content:
Saxo Bank Pricing Overview

Saxo Bank operates an unusual pricing model that appears free on the surface but reveals significant depth when you dig into account requirements and feature access. The Danish investment bank offers three tiers—Classic, Platinum, and VIP—all listed at $0/month, making it technically free to open an account and trade. However, "free" doesn't tell the whole story. Saxo Bank targets professional and institutional traders who manage substantial portfolios, and the real costs emerge through minimum deposit requirements, inactivity fees, and tiered benefits that unlock for traders meeting specific balance or volume thresholds.

All Saxo Bank Plans Compared

Saxo Bank's pricing structure rewards account size and trading activity rather than monthly subscription fees. Here's how the three tiers break down:

Plan Tier Monthly Cost Access Level Target Trader
Classic $0/month Base access to 40,000+ instruments; commission-based trading model Individual retail traders, small account balances
Platinum $0/month Enhanced pricing and priority support; typically requires mid-to-high account balance Active traders managing $50K–$500K+ accounts
VIP $0/month Premium execution, dedicated relationship manager, white-glove support; significant minimum balance requirement Professional and institutional traders, multi-million dollar accounts

All three tiers include access to Saxo Bank's professional platforms (SaxoTraderGO and SaxoTraderPRO), advanced charting, backtesting tools, alerts, mobile apps, full API access for algorithmic trading, custom indicators, automated trading capabilities, performance analytics, risk management tools, news feeds, and educational resources. The primary differences lie in execution pricing, customer support priority, and feature prioritization rather than gated functionality.

Free Plan / Free Trial

Saxo Bank doesn't formally offer a "free tier" in the traditional sense, but the Classic plan effectively functions as a free entry point with no monthly costs. Account opening is free, and you can access paper trading (simulated trading) to practice before risking real capital. Paper trading accounts come fully equipped with real-time data and the same tools as live trading accounts, making it a legitimate way to evaluate the platform's interface, charting capabilities, and execution quality without commitment.

However, there are important caveats. To actively trade live, you'll need to fund your account, and Saxo Bank enforces minimum deposit requirements that vary by account type and region but typically start around $2,000–$10,000 for individual accounts. Traders based in certain jurisdictions may face higher minimums. Additionally, accounts inactive for 12+ months may incur inactivity fees that erode any balance you've left dormant, so a "free" account only remains free if you either trade regularly or withdraw funds.

For traders evaluating whether Saxo Bank is right for them, the paper trading feature is genuinely valuable and worth using before committing capital. It gives you a true sense of the platform's complexity, execution speed, and API integration capabilities without financial risk.

Hidden Costs and Fees

Saxo Bank's transparent monthly pricing masks several costs that can add up:

  • Minimum Deposit Requirements: Account minimums typically range from $2,000 to $10,000+ depending on region and account classification. Institutional accounts and VIP tiers may have substantially higher minimums ($25,000–$500,000+).
  • Trading Commissions: While there are no monthly platform fees, you'll pay per-trade commissions that vary by asset class. Forex spreads are typically competitive (0.6–1.2 pips for major pairs), but stocks, options, and futures carry tiered commissions that decrease with higher volume.
  • Data Feed Costs: Real-time data for certain exchanges (e.g., US stock market Level 2 data, premium news feeds) may carry additional subscription fees, especially for retail traders. Professional subscriptions can range from $50–$300+/month depending on the feeds required.
  • Inactivity Fees: If your account goes inactive for 12+ months, Saxo Bank may charge monthly fees ($10–$25 depending on region and account type) until you resume trading or close the account. This applies to accounts sitting idle with balances.
  • Currency Conversion Fees: Non-base currency transactions incur FX conversion spreads, typically 0.10–0.50% depending on the currency pair.
  • Withdrawal Fees: Some regions and payment methods (wire transfer, bank transfer) may incur small withdrawal fees, though many are waived depending on your tier and account activity.
  • Premium Features: Advanced API usage, dedicated infrastructure for high-frequency trading, and white-glove portfolio management services available to VIP clients carry custom pricing negotiated directly with the bank.

Saxo Bank Pricing vs Competitors

How does Saxo Bank stack up against comparable brokers? Here's a competitive snapshot:

Broker Monthly Cost Minimum Deposit Instruments Strengths
Saxo Bank $0/month (commissions apply) $2,000–$10,000+ 40,000+ Vast asset selection, professional platforms, OpenAPI, multi-asset portfolio management
Interactive Brokers $0/month ($10 minimum monthly commission) $0–$2,000 (depends on account type) 50,000+ Slightly broader instrument library, lower minimums, highly competitive pricing for volume traders
OANDA $0/month $0–$1,000 15,000+ Excellent forex focus, no minimum deposit option, lower barriers to entry for retail traders
IG $0/month $250–$5,000 18,000+ Simple pricing, strong mobile app, good for CFD traders, lower minimums

Value Positioning: Saxo Bank is more expensive than discount brokers like OANDA or IG in terms of minimum deposits and ongoing commissions, but justifies that premium through instrument breadth (40,000+), professional-grade platforms, and robust API access. Interactive Brokers offers comparable breadth and arguably better commission tiering for volume traders, but Saxo Bank's multi-asset strength and OpenAPI integration appeal more to algo traders and quantitative portfolio managers. If you're a casual retail trader, OANDA or IG will be more affordable; if you're a professional managing serious capital and need deep API integration, Saxo Bank becomes competitive.

Is Saxo Bank Worth the Price?

Best For Classic Tier: Traders starting with $2,000–$10,000 who want access to multiple asset classes and don't need VIP support. The $0 monthly cost makes sense if you're committed to active trading; idle accounts will be hit with inactivity fees, so this tier rewards consistent engagement.

Best For Platinum Tier: Active traders managing $50,000–$500,000 accounts who benefit from tighter spreads, faster execution, and better customer support. The upgrade is justified if you're placing 20+ trades/month across multiple asset classes; the execution improvements and tiered commission reductions will exceed the higher minimum balance requirement.

Best For VIP Tier: Institutional traders, portfolio managers, and hedge funds managing multi-million dollar accounts. The dedicated relationship manager, white-glove support, custom API infrastructure, and priority execution order justify the implicit premium. This tier is not cost-competitive with Interactive Brokers on commissions alone, but Saxo Bank's breadth and institutional capabilities fill a specific niche.

Honest Value Assessment: Saxo Bank is not the cheapest broker, but it's not trying to be. You're paying for breadth (40,000+ instruments), professional infrastructure, OpenAPI integration, and regulatory strength. If you trade only forex or stocks, cheaper alternatives exist. If you manage multi-asset portfolios, trade algorithms, or need seamless integration across asset classes, Saxo Bank's $0 monthly cost + commission-based model becomes reasonable.

How to Save on Saxo Bank

  • Reach Higher Tiers: Moving from Classic to Platinum (typically requires $50,000+ balance) unlocks tighter spreads and lower commissions that can save thousands annually on active trading.
  • Leverage Volume Discounts: Commissions decrease on both stocks and derivatives as monthly trade volume increases. High-frequency traders and active traders can negotiate custom commission rates by working with their relationship manager (especially on Platinum and VIP tiers).
  • Minimize Data Feed Costs: Use only essential real-time feeds; Saxo Bank's platform includes delayed quotes for free. For retail traders, delayed data (15–20 minute lag) is often sufficient and eliminates $50–$300/month data subscriptions.
  • Avoid Inactivity Fees: Keep your account active with at least one trade per 12 months, or close/withdraw funds from dormant accounts to sidestep inactivity fees entirely.
  • Use Paper Trading First: Exploit the free paper trading feature extensively before going live. Practicing with real-time data and platform tools costs nothing and prevents expensive beginner mistakes on live capital.
  • Negotiate VIP Terms: If you're managing institutional capital or planning multi-million dollar trades, VIP benefits are negotiable. Contact Saxo Bank's institutional desk directly; custom arrangements on commissions, data feeds, and support can unlock significant savings.

Bottom Line: Saxo Bank's pricing is structured for active, professional traders who value breadth and execution quality over lowest cost. The $0 monthly subscription is genuine, but the real economics depend on account size, trading frequency, and your tolerance for per-trade commissions. For traders moving $50,000+ and planning consistent activity, the platform delivers strong value. For casual traders or those with smaller accounts, cheaper alternatives exist.
